fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility library built out — think of it as the Swiss Army knife we keep wishing we had across projects. Basically every new codebase ends up with someone hand-rolling the same boring stuff: splitting up delimited text, poking at JSON blobs, doing math on strings people paste in, collecting results from a chain of operations, dealing with fixed-position records, making tests not flaky because of real-world clocks, and making sure we don't accidentally log someone's credit card number.

The idea is to expose all of this as clean, reusable contracts so we stop copy-pasting fragile one-offs everywhere. For the test harness piece, it should work similarly to how we set up the runner on the payments service last quarter — same kind of namespaced output folders so different test runs don't stomp on each other.

One thing that keeps biting us: the masking feature needs to handle that 'partial reveal' scenario (you know, show a few characters at the start and end, hide the middle), not just the all-or-nothing case. Also the clock stuff — we need both the kind you nudge manually AND the kind that just ticks itself forward on every read, since different test scenarios need different behavior.

Please make sure the adapter layer stays totally separate from the actual logic — last time someone tangled those together it was a nightmare to maintain. Output should go to stdout only, no metadata mixed in.

trigger_keywords: where do test outputs go
output file naming convention
how are stdout files named and stored
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Test output files must be written to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case in `feature1_csv_records.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` produces `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_csv_records@000.txt`. The index is zero-padded to three digits. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL labels or metadata.

trigger_keywords: default cases directory
what folder does the test runner use by default
cases-dir default value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `--cases-dir` flag defaults to `test_cases` when not supplied. Passing `--cases-dir public_test_cases` points the runner at the public test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/`.

trigger_keywords: partial masking policy
show prefix and suffix
reveal start and end characters while hiding middle
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The masking feature must support at least three policies: `none` (no masking, display value as-is), `hidden` (replace every character with a fixed replacement character, specifically 't'), and a prefix/suffix reveal policy that shows a configured number of leading and trailing characters while masking the middle region. The `display` length must always equal the original value length.

trigger_keywords: hidden mask replacement character
what character replaces hidden values
fully masked output character
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the `hidden` mask policy is applied, every character in the value is replaced with the letter `t`. For example, `hellohello` (10 chars) renders as `tttttttttt` with `length=10`.

trigger_keywords: manual clock vs auto clock
difference between fixed time and auto ticking
when does the clock advance on read
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two clock behaviors. The manually advanced clock (`fixed_time`): reads do NOT advance time; time only moves when a `tick` or `tick:<seconds>` operation is explicitly issued. The auto-advancing clock (`auto_ticking_time`): each `read` operation returns the current instant AND then automatically advances by the configured `defaultTick` before the next read. Both accept the same input shape: `start=<instant>;defaultTick=<seconds>;ops=<comma-separated operations>`.

trigger_keywords: tick operation syntax
how to specify tick amount
explicit tick vs default tick
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A bare `tick` operation advances the clock by the configured `defaultTick` seconds. A `tick:<seconds>` operation advances by the explicitly specified number of seconds, overriding the default for that single operation. `read` operations appear in the output instants list; `tick` operations do not.

trigger_keywords: quoted CSV fields
embedded commas in CSV
escaped quotes in CSV parsing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: CSV parsing must handle quoted fields: a field wrapped in double-quotes may contain embedded commas (treated as field content, not delimiters) and escaped quote sequences. The header is always reported as `none` in the tested cases. Output format is `header=none\nrecords=[[...]]\n`. If parsing cannot consume the entire input, report `error=unconsumed_input` with a normalized diagnostic message.

trigger_keywords: all results fail fast
first failure in sequence
require all to succeed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Feature 4.1 (require all): if every item in the comma-separated sequence is `success:<integer>`, output `status=success
values=[...]
`. If any item is `failure:<reason>`, output `status=failure
` with the FIRST failure reason encountered, stopping there. Feature 4.2 (keep successes): silently drop failures and output only `values=[...]
` with successful integers. Feature 4.3 (partition): output `successes=[...]
` then `failures=[...]
`, preserving encounter order within each group.

trigger_keywords: tuple null padding
missing positions in tuple
trailing nulls in fixed size tuple
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When converting a list to a fixed-size tuple, positions beyond the list's length are padded with `null`. For example, a 4-element list converted to a tuple6 renders as `[1, 2, 3, 4, null, null]`. Feature 5.3 checks a four-position nullable tuple: if all four positions are non-null integers, output `all_non_null=[1, 2, 3, 4]\n`; if any position is null, output `all_non_null=none\n`.

trigger_keywords: arithmetic expression precedence
exponentiation operator priority
operator binding order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Arithmetic expression evaluation must implement the following precedence (tightest to loosest): parentheses override all, then exponentiation, then multiplication and division (left-to-right), then addition and subtraction (left-to-right). Whitespace between tokens is ignored. Successful evaluation outputs `value=<number>\n`. Invalid input that cannot begin a valid expression outputs `error=no_parse` with the original input text.

trigger_keywords: string validation rule names
what rules are supported for strings
named string rules min max exact
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Supported named string validation rules include at minimum: `min10` (length >= 10), `max10` (length <= 10), exact length rules, bounded length rules, non-empty pattern rules, and composed logical rules (e.g., AND/OR combinations). Output format is `valid=<true|false>
rule=<name>
length=<len>
`. For numeric rules the output is `valid=<true|false>
rule=<name>
value=<number>
`. Numeric rules tested include `max10` (value <= 10), `min<N>`, exact value, and inclusive range checks.

trigger_keywords: adapter separation from core
stdin json command routing
execution adapter responsibility
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a JSON command from stdin, routes it to the appropriate core domain logic, and writes only raw stdout output. The core business logic must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The adapter is solely responsible for translating JSON commands into idiomatic method calls. Core logic must never directly depend on I/O implementation details.

trigger_keywords: JSON value normalization
object key rendering order
how are parsed JSON values displayed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parsed JSON values are rendered in a normalized textual form followed by a newline. Supported types: null, booleans, numbers, strings (with escape sequences decoded before rendering), arrays, and objects. Object keys must be rendered deterministically (e.g., sorted). Arrays render as `[1, 2, 3]` style with comma-space separation.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: works similarly to how we set up the runner on the payments service last quarter — same kind of namespaced output folders
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The test harness must namespace stdout output by `<cases-dir>` so that running `--cases-dir public_test_cases` writes to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/` and running a different cases dir writes to a separate subdirectory, preventing cross-run overwrites. See `rcb_tests/test.sh` and the harness spec in start.md.

The masked value feature (Feature 7.3) must support a prefix/suffix reveal policy that exposes a configured number of leading and trailing characters while replacing middle characters, in addition to the `none` and `hidden` policies defined in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature7_3_masked_values.json`.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'd want to check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the spec in `start.md` for the exact contract. If you have a specific scenario in mind, describe the input/output behavior you're expecting and we can align from there.

feature1_csv_records.json.description: Parse CSV text into an optional header and ordered records while preserving quoted field content.

feature2_json_values.json.description: Parse a complete JSON value and render its normalized value form.

feature3_arithmetic_expressions.json.description: Evaluate arithmetic expressions with parentheses, whitespace, exponentiation, multiplication, division, addition, and subtraction precedence.

feature4_1_all_results.json.description: Collect success values from an ordered result sequence only when every item succeeds; otherwise return the first failure reason.

feature4_2_any_successes.json.description: Extract all success values from an ordered result sequence and drop failures.

feature4_3_result_partition.json.description: Separate an ordered result sequence into success values and failure reasons while preserving each group order.

feature5_1_tuple_append.json.description: Append a five-item tuple and a three-item tuple into one ordered eight-item tuple.

feature5_2_tuple_from_list.json.description: Convert an ordered list into fixed-size tuples, preserving available positions and padding missing trailing positions with null.

cases.expected_output:
tuple2=[1, 2]
tuple5=[1, 2, 3, 4, null]
tuple6=[1, 2, 3, 4, null, null]

feature5_3_tuple_nullability.json.description: Convert a nullable four-item tuple into a non-null tuple only when all positions have values.

feature6_1_fixed_time.json.description: Read and manually advance a controllable clock; reading the clock by itself does not advance time.

feature6_2_auto_ticking_time.json.description: Read and manually advance an auto-advancing clock; each read returns the current time and then advances by the configured tick.

feature7_1_string_validation.json.description: Evaluate named string validation rules and report the boolean result together with rule identity and input length.

feature7_2_number_validation.json.description: Evaluate named numeric validation rules and report the boolean result together with rule identity and numeric input.

feature7_3_masked_values.json.description: Render string-backed values using the selected masking policy and report the displayed string length.
